title: VAEs for Anomaly Detection
concept: Like plain autoencoders, VAEs flag anomalies via poor reconstruction, but their probabilistic nature also allows flagging low-likelihood latent codes.

exercises.prompt: A security guard checks if a visitor's badge photo matches their face. What is the guard really doing?

options.text: Comparing the real thing to an expected pattern

exercises.explain: That's exactly how anomaly detection works — compare something to what's 'normal' and see if it matches!

cards.title: Something's Off!
cards.body: Imagine a machine that learned what NORMAL heartbeats, normal factory sounds, or normal selfies look like. When something weird shows up, the machine goes 'wait... that's not right!' That's a VAE catching an anomaly.

cards.title: VAE Anomaly Detection
cards.body: A Variational Autoencoder (VAE) learns to compress normal data into a latent code and rebuild it. If new data reconstructs badly, OR its latent code lands in a weird/unlikely spot, the VAE flags it as an anomaly. It has two ways to catch weirdness, not just one!

cards.title: The Costume Party Bouncer
cards.body: A plain autoencoder bouncer only checks: 'Did your costume come back looking right after I redid it?' A VAE bouncer does that PLUS checks a guest list of normal costume styles — even if the redo looks okay, if your costume style is super rare/unlikely, the VAE bouncer still gets suspicious.

cards.title: Factory Machine Sounds
cards.body: A VAE trained on normal machine hums can rebuild those sounds almost perfectly (low error). But if a machine starts grinding oddly, the VAE struggles to rebuild it well (high reconstruction error) AND the sound's latent code lands far from the usual cluster (low likelihood). Either signal = alarm!

cards.title: Myth: Only reconstruction error matters
cards.body: Truth: A regular autoencoder ONLY checks reconstruction error. But a VAE has a bonus superpower — it also checks if the latent code itself is rare or unlikely in its learned probability space, catching sneaky anomalies that reconstruct 'okay' but land somewhere odd in latent space.

cards.title: Plain Autoencoder vs VAE
cards.body: Both compress and rebuild data, but VAEs add a probability check on the latent code.

left.text: Flags anomalies using ONLY reconstruction error — how bad the rebuild looks

right.text: Flags anomalies using reconstruction error AND unlikely latent codes

exercises.prompt: What extra signal can a VAE use to detect anomalies that a plain autoencoder cannot?

options.text: The color of the training data
How unlikely the latent code is in the learned probability distribution
The size of the neural network

exercises.explain: VAEs learn a probability distribution over latent codes, so they can flag codes that are statistically rare — something plain autoencoders can't do.

exercises.prompt: A VAE can only detect anomalies by looking at reconstruction error, just like a plain autoencoder.
exercises.explain: False! VAEs have a second trick: checking if the latent code is unlikely under the learned distribution, in addition to reconstruction error.

exercises.sentence: A VAE can flag data as an anomaly if it reconstructs poorly OR if its ___ code is very unlikely.
exercises.explain: The 'latent' code is the compressed representation the VAE learns — checking its likelihood is the VAE's special anomaly-detection trick.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario shows a VAE catching an anomaly that a plain autoencoder might completely miss?

options.text: Data reconstructs almost perfectly, but its latent code falls in a very rare, unlikely region
Data reconstructs terribly and looks completely distorted
Data reconstructs perfectly and its latent code is right in the normal cluster

exercises.explain: This is the VAE's special power — since the plain autoencoder only checks reconstruction error, it would miss this anomaly because the reconstruction looked fine!

exercises.prompt: A hospital uses a VAE trained on thousands of normal chest X-rays to detect unusual scans. A new X-ray reconstructs almost perfectly, but its latent code lands in an extremely rare, low-probability region of the latent space. What should the VAE system do?

options.text: Ignore it since reconstruction was good
Flag it as a possible anomaly because of the unlikely latent code, even though reconstruction looked fine
Delete the scan since it's clearly an error
Only trust reconstruction error and never use latent space information

exercises.explain: This is exactly why VAEs are powerful for anomaly detection: they don't rely on reconstruction error alone. An unlikely latent code is its own warning sign, catching subtle anomalies that 'look normal' after rebuilding.
